name: The Decimal Slide
trick: ×10 or ÷10 = slide the digits one place past the decimal point. Never compute — just slide.
why: Each place in a number is worth 10× the place to its right. So ×10 promotes every digit one seat — the digits don't change, their SEATS do.

try.say: The 3 and 6 kept their order — they just moved one seat up.

name: The 10% One-Hop
trick: 10% of anything = slide one place right. 10% of $73 → $7.30. Done.
why: 10% = 10/100 = 1/10 — and dividing by 10 is exactly one slide right. The tip trick is just the decimal slide wearing a percent costume.

try.say: One hop. This works on every bill you will ever see.

name: The Double-Tax Tip
trick: 20% tip = find 10% (one slide), then double it. On $65: slide → $6.50, double → $13.
why: 20% is just 10% twice. Two easy moves beat one hard one — every time.

try.say: Slide to $6.50, double to $13. You're now faster than the adults at the table.

name: The Costume Rule
trick: Every fraction is a division in costume: 3/4 = 3 ÷ 4 = 0.75. And “of” means ×. So 3/4 of 20 = 20 × 3 ÷ 4 = 15.
why: The fraction bar and the ÷ sign are the SAME symbol drawn differently. That's why fractions, decimals and percents convert into each other — they were one number all along, just changing outfits.

name: The Half-Then-Ten
trick: ×5 = halve the number, then ×10. 48 × 5: half is 24, slide up → 240.
why: 5 is just 10 ÷ 2. So multiplying by 5 = multiplying by 10 and halving — in whichever order is easier.

try.say: Half of 48 is 24, one slide up: 240. No carrying, no sweat.

name: The Almost-Ten
why: 9 = 10 − 1, so 47 × 9 = 47×10 − 47×1. That's the distributive property — the same one from algebra class — doing your arithmetic for you.

try.say: 470 minus 47. The distributive property just bought you dessert.

name: The Eleven Spread
trick: ×11: keep the first and last digits, fill the middle with sums of neighbors. 1234 × 11 → 1, 1+2, 2+3, 3+4, 4 → 13574.
why: 11 = 10 + 1, so N × 11 = N slid one place PLUS N itself. When you add a number to its own slid copy, each digit lands on its neighbor — THAT'S why neighbors add. Not magic: place value.

try.say: 3, then 3+5=8, then 5. It's just 350 + 35 in disguise.

name: The Even Trade
trick: Stuck on a × problem? Double one side, halve the other, repeat until easy: 16 × 25 → 8 × 50 → 4 × 100 = 400.
why: Doubling one factor and halving the other is ×2 and ÷2 at once — they cancel, so the answer can't change. You're just trading the problem for an easier twin.

try.say: Trade twice and it becomes 4 × 100. The seesaw principle, but for multiplication.
